quotes.verbatim_quote: This pattern is based on the fragments 1-2-3-5 of EACH chord scale.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Defines the foundational scalar fragment rule that drives Pattern 125 and establishes the method's approach of extracting melodic cells from chord scales.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
This pattern is based on chord tones of the respective chords shown. Arabic numbers indicate the
succession of tones from each chord, necessary to complete this pattern in all keys.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Establishes the chord-tone (rather than scalar) derivation method used in Pattern 126, contrasting it with the scale-fragment approach of surrounding patterns.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
This pattern is based on two sets of fragments which are to be extracted from the scales of the illus-
trated chords. The fragment 3-4-5-7 is to be extracted from the scale of the minor 7th chord, and
the fragment 3-4-5-1 is to be extracted from the scale of the dominant seventh chord. Practice Pat-
tern No. 127 with the chords in Patterns No. 123-126.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Defines a two-fragment substitution rule assigning different scale extracts to each chord quality, the core technique repeated across Patterns 127–134.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
Patterns No. 135-138 will deal with the IIm7-V7-IM7 progression in ewo measures. Rather than in-
troducing new patterns, we will use four of the patterns previously discussed with the IIm7-V7 pro-
gression and extend them to the IM7. The student can then apply any of the remaining [m7-V7
(one measure) progressions to the chords in Patterns No. 135-138, and experiment with extending
them to the IM7 chord.
quotes.why_load_bearing: States the method's compositional instruction: recycle mastered one-measure fragments by extending them to IM7, teaching generative economy rather than rote new patterns.

quotes.verbatim_quote: This pattern uses the scales of the illustrated chords, ascending the scale of the minor seventh chord and descending the scale of the dominant seventh chord.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Defines a directional (ascending/descending) scale-assignment rule for the two-measure IIm7-V7, and the following practice variations make it the most systematically developed pattern in the chapter.

quotes.topic: Shared parent key / common scale rule
quotes.verbatim_quote: This pattern uses the scale of the minor seventh chord (in 3rds) which is continued for two measures, using eighth notes. In this manner, it ends on the seventh tone of the dominant seventh chord, as illustrated. The pattern fits both chords because they both have the same parent key. Consequently, the accidentals in their respective scales are the same. Also, the sequence of pitches in the measure containing the dominant seventh chord establishes the sonority of that chord.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Articulates the harmonic rationale — shared parent key — that justifies running a single scale across both the IIm7 and V7 chords, the theoretical foundation for this family of patterns.
